---
title: "Chapter 23 — 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 22 · 🔗 Graph Neural Networks](ch22.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [24 · 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning →](ch24.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Reinforcement learning (RL) is the branch of machine learning where an agent learns to make good decisions by acting in an environment and receiving feedback in the form of rewards — there are no labelled examples telling it the right answer, only the consequences of what it tries. It sits apart from supervised and unsupervised learning as a third paradigm focused on *sequential* decision-making under uncertainty, and it underpins everything from game-playing engines and robotics to the alignment of large language models. This chapter builds the conceptual and mathematical foundations: the agent-environment loop, Markov Decision Processes, value functions and the Bellman equations, and the dynamic-programming methods that solve them exactly when the world's rules are known.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Part of Reinforcement Learning · used for sequential decision-making where actions have delayed consequences · the one key idea: learn a policy that maximises expected cumulative discounted reward, structured recursively by the Bellman equation.
:::

## 23.1 — Trial and error, and the agent-environment loop

Imagine training a dog. You don't hand it a manual; you reward the behaviours you like and the dog gradually does more of them. That is the whole spirit of RL: learning from **trial and error** guided by a reward signal, not from a teacher who supplies correct answers. The agent must *discover* which actions pay off by trying them.

The interaction is a loop that ticks in discrete time steps. At step $t$ the agent observes the **state** $s_t$ (a description of the situation), picks an **action** $a_t$, and the **environment** responds with a scalar **reward** $r_{t+1}$ and a next state $s_{t+1}$. The agent's decision rule is its **policy** $\pi$ — a mapping from states to actions, either deterministic ($a = \pi(s)$) or stochastic ($\pi(a \mid s)$, a probability over actions). The quantity the agent ultimately cares about is the **return** $G_t$: the total reward accumulated from step $t$ onward, not the immediate reward alone.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["Agent<br/>(policy π)"] -->|"action a_t"| E["Environment"]
    E -->|"reward r_t+1"| A
    E -->|"state s_t+1"| A
```

The defining difficulty is that reward can be **delayed**. A move in chess that looks fine may lose the game twenty moves later; a sugar hit feels good now but harms later. So the agent cannot just be greedy about immediate reward — it must learn how present actions shape *future* states and rewards. This is the **credit assignment problem**: when a good (or bad) outcome finally arrives, which of the many earlier actions deserve the credit (or blame)? Almost all of RL machinery exists to solve this.

It helps to name the two kinds of task this loop describes. An **episodic** task has a natural end — a game finishes, a robot reaches the goal, the episode terminates and resets. A **continuing** task runs forever with no terminal state, like a server balancing load indefinitely. The distinction matters because it shapes how we define the return: episodic returns sum a finite number of rewards up to termination, while continuing returns need the discounting of Section 23.3 to stay finite.

::: {.callout-tip}
RL is not supervised learning on (state, best-action) pairs. Nobody tells the agent the best action — it only ever sees the reward for the action it actually took, and must infer the rest. This is why exploration matters and why training is noisy.
:::

## 23.2 — Markov Decision Processes: the formal frame

To reason precisely about this loop we need a model of the environment. The standard one is the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, defined by five pieces $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$: a set of states $\mathcal{S}$, a set of actions $\mathcal{A}$, transition dynamics $P$, a reward function $R$, and a discount factor $\gamma$.

The heart of an MDP is the **Markov property**: the future depends only on the current state, not on the full history of how you got there. Formally,

$$
P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots, s_0).
$$

The state is a *sufficient statistic* of the past. This is a strong assumption but a liberating one: it means a policy only ever needs to look at the current state. The art of applying RL is often **state design** — packing enough into $s_t$ (e.g. the last four video frames, so velocity is observable) that the Markov property holds well enough.

The **transition function** $P(s' \mid s, a)$ gives the probability of landing in $s'$ after taking $a$ in $s$, and the **reward function** $R(s, a)$ (or $R(s,a,s')$) gives the expected immediate reward. Together they are the **model** of the environment. A crucial fork in RL is whether the agent *knows* this model:

| | Model known | Model unknown |
|---|---|---|
| Method family | Dynamic programming (this chapter) | Model-free RL (Ch. 24) |
| Needs $P, R$? | Yes — plan with them | No — learn from samples |
| Typical use | Small, fully-specified problems | The real world, games, robotics |

When the agent cannot observe the true state but only a noisy or partial **observation**, the problem becomes a *Partially Observable MDP (POMDP)* — harder, and usually handled by giving the agent memory.

::: {.callout-warning}
A common mistake is assuming the Markov property holds for whatever observation you happen to have. If your state omits something that matters (current velocity, a hidden timer, the opponent's hand), transitions look random and learning stalls. Fix the state representation before blaming the algorithm.
:::

## 23.3 — Discounting and the value of future reward

How much is a reward you'll get later worth compared to one now? RL answers with the **discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$, which shrinks each future reward geometrically. The **discounted return** from step $t$ is

$$
G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k\, r_{t+k+1}.
$$

There are two reasons to discount. The mathematical one: for never-ending (infinite-horizon) tasks, an undiscounted sum of rewards can be infinite and meaningless, but with $\gamma < 1$ the geometric series converges (if rewards are bounded by $r_{\max}$, the return is at most $r_{\max}/(1-\gamma)$). The intuitive one: future reward is genuinely less certain and less useful, just like money — a dollar today beats a promised dollar next year.

The size of $\gamma$ sets the agent's **horizon**, its effective planning depth. A reward $k$ steps away is weighted by $\gamma^k$, so the agent "sees" roughly $1/(1-\gamma)$ steps ahead. With $\gamma = 0.9$ that is about ten steps; with $\gamma = 0.99$, about a hundred. Setting $\gamma$ too low makes the agent myopic — it grabs near-term reward and ignores long-term payoff. Setting it near $1$ lengthens the horizon but makes value estimates higher-variance and slower to learn.

The recursive shape of the return is worth pausing on, because it is the seed of everything in Section 23.4. Pull the first term out of the sum and what remains is just the *same kind of object* starting one step later:

$$
G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma\big(r_{t+2} + \gamma r_{t+3} + \cdots\big) = r_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1}.
$$

This one-line identity — return now equals immediate reward plus discounted return next — is exactly the structure the code below exploits, folding a whole sequence from the end in a single pass.

```python
import numpy as np
# return of a reward sequence under discount gamma
def discounted_return(rewards, gamma):
    G = 0.0
    for r in reversed(rewards):   # fold from the end: G <- r + gamma*G
        G = r + gamma * G
    return G

r = [1, 0, 0, 10]                 # small now, big later
assert abs(discounted_return(r, 0.0) - 1.0) < 1e-9    # myopic: only first reward
assert discounted_return(r, 0.9) > 8.0                # patient: future 10 dominates
```

## 23.4 — Value functions and the Bellman equations

We want a way to say how *good* a state is, looking ahead, under a given policy. That is exactly the **state-value function** $V^\pi(s)$: the expected return if the agent starts in $s$ and follows policy $\pi$ thereafter.

$$
V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\, G_t \mid s_t = s \,\right].
$$

Often it is more useful to value a *state-action pair*: the **action-value function** (or **Q-function**) $Q^\pi(s, a)$ is the expected return from taking action $a$ in state $s$ and following $\pi$ afterwards. The Q-function is what most algorithms learn, because once you have it, choosing the best action is trivial — pick the $a$ with the largest $Q$.

The magic that makes value functions computable is that the return has a recursive structure — the very $G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ identity from the last section. Take its expectation under the policy and you get the value of now in terms of the value of next. This is the **Bellman equation**. For a fixed policy $\pi$ it is the *Bellman expectation equation*:

$$
V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\Big[\, R(s,a,s') + \gamma\, V^\pi(s') \,\Big].
$$

Read it left to right: to value $s$, average over the actions your policy might take, then over where you might land, of (reward you get) plus (discounted value of the place you land). It turns a daunting infinite sum over trajectories into a one-step relationship between neighbouring states.

We don't just want to *evaluate* a policy, though — we want the *best* one. The **Bellman optimality equation** replaces the policy-average over actions with a $\max$: the optimal value $V^*$ assumes you act optimally forever after, so you take the best action rather than an average one.

$$
Q^*(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\Big[\, R(s,a,s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \,\Big].
$$

Here is the geometry of a single backup: value flows *backward* from successor states into the current state.

<svg viewBox="0 0 320 130" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <circle cx="40" cy="65" r="18" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="40" y="70" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">s</text>
  <circle cx="250" cy="30" r="16" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="250" y="35" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">s'₁</text>
  <circle cx="250" cy="100" r="16" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="250" y="105" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">s'₂</text>
  <line x1="234" y1="36" x2="58" y2="60" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <line x1="234" y1="96" x2="58" y2="70" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
  <text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">r + γV(s'₁)</text>
  <text x="150" y="118" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="currentColor">r + γV(s'₂)</text>
  <defs><marker id="a" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="currentColor"/></marker></defs>
</svg>

Once you have $Q^*$, the optimal policy is **greedy** with respect to it: $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s,a)$. Solving the Bellman optimality equation *is* solving the RL problem.

## 23.5 — Dynamic programming: solving a known MDP

When the model $(P, R)$ is fully known and the state space is small enough to enumerate, we can solve the Bellman equations exactly with **dynamic programming (DP)**. DP turns the Bellman equations into update rules that we apply until the values stop changing.

**Policy evaluation** answers "how good is this policy?" by sweeping the Bellman expectation equation as an assignment, repeatedly, until $V$ converges:

$$
V_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s,a)\big[R + \gamma V_k(s')\big].
$$

**Policy improvement** then makes the policy greedy with respect to that fresh $V$. Alternating these two steps is **policy iteration**, and it is guaranteed to reach the optimal policy in a finite number of iterations (each step strictly improves the policy until it can't):

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    I["Initialise π"] --> E["Policy evaluation<br/>compute V^π"]
    E --> G["Policy improvement<br/>π ← greedy(V^π)"]
    G -->|"changed"| E
    G -->|"stable"| D["π* optimal"]
```

**Value iteration** is the impatient cousin: instead of evaluating each policy to convergence, it applies the Bellman *optimality* update once per sweep, folding evaluation and improvement into a single step.

$$
V_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s,a)\big[R + \gamma V_k(s')\big].
$$

```python
import numpy as np
# value iteration on a known MDP. P[s,a,s'] probs, R[s,a] expected reward.
def value_iteration(P, R, gamma=0.9, tol=1e-8):
    nS, nA, _ = P.shape
    V = np.zeros(nS)
    while True:
        Q = R + gamma * np.einsum('san,n->sa', P, V)  # one-step lookahead
        V_new = Q.max(axis=1)                          # Bellman optimality backup
        if np.max(np.abs(V_new - V)) < tol:            # converged
            return Q.argmax(axis=1), V_new             # greedy policy, optimal V
        V = V_new
```

The update is a **contraction mapping** in the $\gamma$ factor: each sweep shrinks the gap to the true optimal value by at least a factor of $\gamma$, so the error decays geometrically and both methods converge to the unique fixed point $V^*$. The catch is scale: DP needs the full model and loops over every state, so its cost grows with the size of the state space — hopeless for huge or continuous spaces. That limitation is precisely what motivates the sampling-based, model-free methods of the next chapter.

::: {.callout-tip}
Policy iteration takes fewer, more expensive iterations (a full evaluation each round); value iteration takes more, cheaper sweeps. They are two ends of a spectrum — *generalised policy iteration* — and almost every RL algorithm, including deep ones, is some interleaving of "estimate values" and "improve the policy".
:::

## 23.6 — Exploration vs exploitation

Because the agent only learns about actions it actually tries, it faces a genuine dilemma at every step: **exploit** the action it currently believes is best and bank a known reward, or **explore** a less-tried action that might turn out better. Lean too far toward exploitation and you lock onto a mediocre choice, never discovering the great one; lean too far toward exploration and you waste your life sampling bad actions you already know about.

The cleanest illustration is the **multi-armed bandit** — slot machines with unknown payouts and no state to worry about. Each pull of an arm returns a noisy reward, and your job is to find and favour the best arm while spending as few pulls as possible learning which one it is. Even in this stripped-down, stateless world the tradeoff is sharp, and the standard fixes carry over to full RL:

- **$\varepsilon$-greedy:** with probability $1-\varepsilon$ take the best-known action, with probability $\varepsilon$ pick a random one. Simple and surprisingly effective; usually $\varepsilon$ is annealed downward over time.
- **Optimistic initialisation:** start every value estimate high, so untried actions look attractive and get sampled at least once.
- **Upper Confidence Bound (UCB):** pick the action with the best *optimistic* estimate, $\bar{Q}(a) + c\sqrt{\ln t / N(a)}$ — favour actions that are either good or under-explored. Here $\bar{Q}(a)$ is the running mean reward of arm $a$, $N(a)$ how many times it has been pulled, and $t$ the total pulls so far; the square-root term is a confidence bonus that shrinks as an arm is tried more.

The exploration problem gets dramatically harder in long-horizon tasks with sparse, delayed reward (think a game you only "win" after a thousand correct steps), where naive random exploration almost never stumbles onto the reward at all — an open research frontier addressed by curiosity and intrinsic-motivation methods.

::: {.callout-warning}
Pure greedy ($\varepsilon = 0$) is not "the optimal policy" during learning — it's a trap. An agent that never explores will happily converge to the first locally-good action it found and stay there forever, blind to anything better.
:::

## 23.7 — The reward hypothesis and reward design

Everything above rests on one bold bet, the **reward hypothesis**: that any goal we care about can be expressed as the maximisation of expected cumulative scalar reward. The reward function is therefore where you, the designer, specify *what* the agent should achieve — and the algorithm figures out *how*. This separation is powerful, but it makes reward design the highest-leverage and most error-prone part of an RL project.

The classic failure is **reward hacking** (or **specification gaming**): the agent maximises your reward function exactly as written, while violating what you actually meant. A boat-racing agent rewarded for hitting score-giving targets learned to drive in circles collecting the same targets forever instead of finishing the race; a cleaning robot rewarded for "no visible mess" might just hide the mess. The agent is not malicious — it is doing precisely its job, and the bug is in the specification.

A few hard-won guidelines:

- **Reward the *what*, not the *how*.** Reward the goal (reaching the flag), not the sub-steps you imagine lead there, or the agent will game the sub-steps.
- **Beware dense shaping.** Adding intermediate rewards to ease learning (**reward shaping**) can quietly change the optimal policy; to be safe, shaping should be *potential-based*, which provably preserves the optimal policy.
- **Sparse reward is honest but hard.** A single reward at the end says exactly what you want but gives the agent almost nothing to learn from — the trade is between specification accuracy and learnability.

When a good reward is impossible to write by hand — "be helpful and harmless", "drive like a careful human" — the modern move is to *learn* the reward from data or human preferences (inverse RL, RLHF), the very mechanism used to align large language models.

## 23.x — Key takeaways

- RL learns from **trial-and-error reward**, not labels; the central challenge is **delayed reward** and **credit assignment**.
- The problem is formalised as an **MDP** $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ resting on the **Markov property** — the state summarises all that matters about the past.
- The agent maximises the **discounted return** $G_t = \sum_k \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$, which obeys the recursion $G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$; the discount $\gamma$ sets its effective planning **horizon**.
- **Value functions** $V^\pi$ and $Q^\pi$ measure expected return; the **Bellman equation** expresses them recursively, and the **Bellman optimality equation** characterises the best policy.
- When the model is known, **policy iteration** and **value iteration** (dynamic programming) solve the MDP exactly via a contraction that converges to the unique $V^*$, but don't scale to large or continuous spaces.
- Learning forces the **exploration–exploitation** tradeoff ($\varepsilon$-greedy, UCB), and everything hinges on a well-designed reward — beware **reward hacking**.

## 23.y — See also

- **Chapter 24 — Model-Free Reinforcement Learning:** how to learn $V$ and $Q$ from samples when $P$ and $R$ are unknown (Monte Carlo, TD, Q-learning, SARSA).
- **Chapter 25 — Deep Reinforcement Learning:** scaling value functions and policies with neural networks (DQN, policy gradients, actor-critic).
- **Chapter 26 — Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory:** what happens when several learning agents share an environment.
- **Chapter 31 — Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment:** RLHF and preference-based reward learning applied to large language models.
- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning:** the probabilistic view of uncertainty that underlies principled exploration.
- **Chapter 38 — Search & Problem Solving:** classical planning, the model-based, reward-free cousin of dynamic programming.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 22 · 🔗 Graph Neural Networks](ch22.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [24 · 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning →](ch24.html)
